title: How Browsers Render Pages
intro: Your team's website loads but shows a blank white screen for 3 seconds before the content appears. Just like a house can't be painted until the walls are up, a browser follows strict steps to build a page. Users are complaining, and you need to understand how browsers render pages to find the bottleneck.

teach.title: Rendering = Building a House
teach.body: A browser renders a page like building a house. First the **frame** goes up (HTML structure). Then the **paint and wallpaper** (CSS styling). Then the **furniture and appliances** (JavaScript interactivity). If any step is blocked, the whole build pauses -- just like a house can't be painted until the walls are up.

teach.title: Rendering Is Like Assembling IKEA Furniture
teach.body: When you build IKEA furniture, you follow steps in order: first the frame, then the shelves, then the doors. If a crucial piece is missing from the box, you're stuck waiting until you find it. Browsers work the same way -- they follow a strict order (HTML, then CSS, then paint), and if a file hasn't arrived yet, everything after it waits. A missing CSS file is like a missing shelf bracket -- nothing looks right until it shows up.

teach.title: The Rendering Pipeline
teach.body:
When your browser receives HTML, it follows these steps:

1. **Parse HTML** -- builds the DOM (Document Object Model), a tree of elements
2. **Parse CSS** -- figures out what styles apply to each element
3. **Layout** -- calculates the size and position of every element
4. **Paint** -- draws pixels to the screen
5. **Composite** -- layers everything together

If CSS or JavaScript files are still loading, steps 2-5 can be blocked.

teach.title: Render-Blocking Resources
teach.body:
Some files **block rendering** -- the browser won't paint anything until they're loaded:

- **CSS files** block rendering because the browser won't show unstyled content
- **JavaScript in the `<head>`** blocks parsing because it might modify the page

That's why large CSS files or slow-loading scripts cause the dreaded 'white screen' while the page loads.

Speed is money. Studies show that every extra second of load time loses customers. When you vibecode with rendering performance in mind, your apps feel instant -- and fast apps convert visitors into paying users.

Browsers parse HTML into a DOM, apply CSS styles, calculate layouts, and paint pixels. CSS and JavaScript files can block this process, causing slow page loads. Understanding this pipeline helps you fix performance problems.

start.narrative: Users report a 3-second white screen before your website appears. You open Chrome DevTools and check the Network tab. You see that a 2MB JavaScript file (bundle.js) is loading in the <head> of the page. This file takes 2.8 seconds to download. What's the problem?

choices.text: The JavaScript file in the <head> is blocking the browser from rendering anything
The server is too slow to respond
The HTML file itself is too large

wrong-server.narrative: The Network tab shows the server responds in 200ms -- that's fast. The delay is on the client side: the browser is waiting for a large JavaScript file to finish downloading before it renders anything. The file is the bottleneck, not the server.

choices.text: So the JavaScript file in the <head> is blocking the render

wrong-html.narrative: The HTML file is only 15KB and loads in 100ms. The problem is the 2MB JavaScript file referenced in the <head> -- the browser stops parsing while it downloads and runs that script.

choices.text: Got it -- the script in the <head> is the render-blocking culprit

fix-blocking.narrative: Step 1 of the pipeline (parse HTML to build the DOM) is blocked by the script. How do you unblock it so the browser can build the DOM in parallel with the script download?

choices.text: Add the 'defer' attribute: <script defer src='bundle.js'> -- script downloads in parallel and runs after DOM is built
Add 'async' instead -- runs the moment it downloads, even if that interrupts HTML parsing
Remove JavaScript from the page entirely

css-step.narrative: DOM construction is now smooth. But the next pipeline step -- building the CSSOM and applying styles -- is still slow. A single 800KB CSS file in the <head> blocks paint until it finishes downloading. What do you do?

choices.text: Inline the critical above-the-fold CSS in <style> and load the rest with media queries or rel='preload'
Split the stylesheet by route and only ship the CSS that page needs
Leave the CSS as is — users will get used to the wait

layout-step.narrative: The page paints quickly now. But scrolling feels janky on a long product list — every scroll event triggers layout recalculation because your code reads element.offsetHeight in a loop and then writes new styles. What's the fix?

choices.text: Batch all reads first, then all writes — avoid the read/write/read/write cycle that forces synchronous layouts
Wrap the writes in requestAnimationFrame so they run before the next paint
Throttle the scroll handler to 100ms — fewer recalcs, but laggy feedback

paint-step.narrative: Layout is stable. The final pipeline step is paint and composite. You have a sticky header that re-paints on every scroll, dragging the framerate down to 30fps. How do you keep paint cost low?

choices.text: Promote the header to its own compositor layer with transform: translateZ(0) or will-change: transform — the GPU handles the move with no repaint
Animate top/left in JS — those properties happen to trigger layout and paint every frame

excellent-end.narrative: You walked the full pipeline: unblocked DOM construction with defer, inlined critical CSS so paint started fast, batched reads/writes so layout stayed cheap, and promoted the sticky header to a GPU layer so scroll hit 60fps. The white screen is gone and the page feels instant.
excellent-end.feedback: This is rendering performance done right. Each fix targeted a specific pipeline stage: parse, style, layout, paint. When users say 'the site feels fast,' this is what they're feeling.

mediocre-end.narrative: The page paints fast on first load, but scrolling still re-lays out every frame because the header animation hits paint-heavy properties. Performance scores are mixed.
mediocre-end.feedback: You handled the load pipeline well, but paint is its own discipline. Animate transform and opacity (compositor-only) instead of top/left/width/height (which force layout and paint).

weak-end.narrative: Some pipeline stages are tuned, but you accepted a janky scroll. Users on lower-end phones drop the page within seconds.
weak-end.feedback: 30fps feels broken to the human eye, especially on mobile. The fixes for paint cost are cheap — at minimum promote the animated element to its own layer so scroll stays smooth.
